Scenario.overview: Water and Wastewater Systems Complex A sophisticated cyber attack orchestrated by the expert attackers behind the Pikabot Distribution February 2024 campaign aims to infiltrate and disrupt critical water and wastewater systems. Utilizing a multi-stage approach, they leverage advanced techniques to gain unauthorized access, maintain persistence, escalate privileges, evade defenses, and ultimately impact the operational integrity of the targeted infrastructure.
Scenario.attack_surface: AVAILABLE_SURFACES: Internet-facing assets, Network Traffic, File, Application Log, Network Traffic, Target System Artifact, Module
Scenario.attack_vector: AVAILABLE_VECTORS: Delivery Method, Network Traffic Flow (Mechanism), Command Execution (Mechanism), File Modification (Mechanism), Application Log Content (Mechanism), Network Traffic Flow (Mechanism), Exfiltration Over C2 Channel, Module Load (Mechanism)

stage.name: Reconnaissance
stage.capec: CAPEC ID: 309 Name: Network Topology Mapping
stage.description: The attackers engage in active scanning activities to map out the network topology of the water and wastewater systems, identifying potential entry points and vulnerabilities.

stage.name: Resource Development
stage.capec: CAPEC ID: 522 Name: Malicious Hardware Component Replacement
stage.description: The attackers establish servers for command and control (C2) and data exfiltration, setting up the necessary infrastructure for their operation.

stage.name: Initial Access
stage.description: Using the Pikabot distribution method from February 2024, attackers send phishing emails to trick employees into opening malicious attachments, leading to Initial Access.

stage.name: Execution
stage.capec: CAPEC ID: 663 Name: Exploitation of Transient Instruction Execution
stage.description: Once the malicious attachment is opened, it triggers the execution of Pikabot malware.

stage.name: Persistence
stage.capec: CAPEC ID: 532 Name: Altered Installed BIOS
stage.description: To maintain persistent access, attackers modify system images to install Pikabot or similar backdoors.

stage.name: Privilege Escalation
stage.capec: CAPEC ID: 121 Name: Exploit Non-Production Interfaces
stage.description: Attackers exploit public-facing applications to escalate privileges within the compromised system.

stage.name: Defense Evasion
stage.capec: CAPEC ID: 636 Name: Hiding Malicious Data or Code within Files
stage.description: Attackers obfuscate files and information to avoid detection by security tools.

stage.name: Credential Access
stage.capec: CAPEC ID: 226 Name: Session Credential Falsification through Manipulation
stage.description: By utilizing tools like Mimikatz, attackers steal credentials to move laterally without detection.

stage.name: Discovery
stage.capec: CAPEC ID: 309 Name: Network Topology Mapping
stage.description: Understanding the network layout, attackers plan lateral movements to reach critical assets.

stage.name: Lateral Movement
stage.capec: CAPEC ID: 324 Name: TCP (ISN) Sequence Predictability Probe
stage.description: Utilizing remote services like RDP or SSH, attackers move throughout the network to extend their control.

stage.name: Collection
stage.capec: CAPEC ID: 328 Name: TCP 'RST' Flag Checksum Probe
stage.description: Attackers collect sensitive operational data from local systems to further their objectives.

stage.name: Exfiltration
stage.capec: CAPEC ID: 24 Name: Filter Failure through Buffer Overflow
stage.description: Collected data is transferred to attacker-controlled servers via established C2 channels.

stage.name: Impact
stage.capec: CAPEC ID: 276 Name: Inter-component Protocol Manipulation
stage.description: Finally, attackers disrupt operations by manipulating inter-process communication, potentially causing physical damage or data loss.

Scenario.summary: The Pikabot Distribution February 2024 campaign represents a highly sophisticated and multi-faceted cyber threat targeting water and wastewater systems. With a focus on stealth and persistence, the attackers employ a wide range of techniques to achieve their goals, from initial infiltration to the ultimate impact on system operations. Organizations in the water sector must remain vigilant and implement robust security measures to mitigate such threats.
